title: An analysis I spent 3 years on understanding Eva or the gospel of a new century
display_title: An analysis I spent 3 years on understanding Eva or the gospel of a new century
wikitext:
[]: An analysis I spent 3 years on: understanding Eva, or the gospel of a new century             Quote Post by Protohipster » Tue Dec 24, 2019 5:24 pm No abstract discussions, comparisons to philosophers, psychologists, religions, etc. Just intensive analysis relying mostly on cinematography and motifs, further interpreted by Anno’s own life and the series’ context. For those willing to invest an hour in an intensive anime analysis video, hope you enjoy! Criticism is appreciated! https://youtu.be/pjngWJ3MBQ4

[]: Re: An analysis I spent 3 years on: understanding Eva, or the gospel of a new century             Quote Post by LPeyrani » Wed Dec 25, 2019 5:58 am Perfect X-Mas surprise present, thank you. I'm going to watch it right now.

[]: Re: An analysis I spent 3 years on: understanding Eva, or the gospel of a new century             Quote Post by LPeyrani » Wed Dec 25, 2019 7:43 am Well, the contents are good, but there is a problem with editing (IMO). The rhythm is both fast and flat, which makes it kind of difficult to follow after a while. I think it would be nice if you slowed the pace down (consider pausing!), used less layers simultaneously and (I know this would be a lot of work) sometimes showed your face as you're talking, so that lip-reading (and facial emotion expression recognition) helped in following your train of thoughts. Anyway, your analysis is deep and you do posit issues I never considered. You did a lot of work, and you deserve a loud CONGRATULATIONS! PS: where did you find reference to a nazi eugenetist named "Konrad Lawrene"? Aren't you referring to Konrad Lorenz, the most important ethologist in history and winner of the Nobel Prize? If that's so, go correct that bit because it greatly reduces your persuasiveness as lecturer.

PS: where did you find reference to a nazi eugenetist named "Konrad Lawrene"? Aren't you referring to Konrad Lorenz, the most important ethologist in history and winner of the Nobel Prize? If that's so, go correct that bit because it greatly reduces your persuasiveness as lecturer.
